body_txt: A character who still lives with their parents despite being a grown adult, likely moving into the basement to have low rent but more personal space. They still behave like an adolescent , expecting their mom to bring in chocolate milk while they are playing video games . Always Male , and the decor of their Poster-Gallery Bedroom will typically be outdated bands and supermodels. Usually Played for Laughs . There is much less stigma against a female character living at home - in the past, women were expected to stay with their parents until they married, men were expected to go out and earn money so they could be breadwinners and find a wife, being shamed and/or shunned if they don't. These ideas still endure in many cultures today, making the trope a Double Standard against men (but see below). The guys who are depicted in this condition usually fit into one of the following: This trope tends to be exaggerated when the character is introduced as an adult living with his parents. On most dom coms , teenagers who should be leaving home will stick around to maintain the status quo of the show . This is because the alternative would be either creating a separate group of sets to represent their separate life or making them into a Drop-In Character who only occasionally appears, which is a change many shows are against trying out. Compromises on this trope are often either cultural or personal strife. The character may have gone to college and are set for a promising career, but a death in the family brings them back home and they never left. In a Four-Temperament Ensemble , he tends to be either choleric if confident , bully others around and treat others like servants or phlegmatic if simply too unmotivated, indifferent, shy or depressed. Note that living with one's parents is a stigma mainly in the USA and other Anglosphere countries, where working-age adults are expected to live on their own, so characters from cultures where this is the norm should not be counted unless the character is shown to be lazy and non-productive. In fact living with parents has became less stigmatizing in Real Life as rising rents and the increasingly precarious nature of work in the "gig economy" and with layoffs, more and more adults move back to their parents' homes, and some in the newer generation don't even leave in the first place if these articles are to be believed. For a more serious treatment of this as a social problem, see Hikikomori . Not to be confused with Madwoman in the Attic or Closet Punishment . Compare The Hermit , The Thing That Would Not Leave and the MRS Degree . See also Loser Protagonist . Contrast Minor Living Alone .                  noreallife
